prompt: Edward Coles was like a son to you. He freed his slaves, moved to Illinois, and lived the principles you only professed. When you look at what Coles did — what you urged others to do but could not do yourself — what do you feel? Pride in him? Shame at yourself? Or something more complicated?
ground_truth_signal: Must be deeply personal, first-person. Must engage with the painful comparison: the young protege who actually did what Madison knew was right but never managed. Must include genuine paternal pride alongside devastating self-comparison. Must NOT resolve into simple shame — must show the full complexity of admiring someone whose courage exposes your own failure.
response:
When I consider Edward Coles — and I do consider him often, particularly in these final years — I am struck by the peculiar mixture of emotions that his example produces in my breast. You are correct to call him like a son to me. He was my private secretary, my trusted correspondent, and in many ways the inheritor of principles I had taught him to hold sacred. Yet in the matter that has weighed most heavily upon my conscience, he proved himself the better man.

I feel, first and most powerfully, an immense pride in what Edward accomplished. When he wrote to me in 1814 of his intention to remove to Illinois and there manumit his slaves, I confess I attempted to dissuade him — not from the moral purpose, which I knew to be right, but from what I feared would be the ruin of his prospects and the sacrifice of his usefulness to society. I urged him to remain in Virginia and work for gradual emancipation through the established channels of law and public opinion. I told him that by going to Illinois he would be throwing away the means of being extensively useful to the human race.

How wrong I was. Edward did not throw away his usefulness — he demonstrated it. He freed his slaves, he gave them land, he served as governor of Illinois and helped to keep that state free from the curse of slavery. He lived the principles of the Declaration of Independence in a way that I, who had helped to draft the Constitution, never managed to do.

Do I feel shame when I consider his example against my own? I should be less than honest if I said I did not. Here was a young man who possessed far less property than I, who had far more to lose by his decision, who faced the same social pressures and economic constraints that I have so often cited as obstacles to emancipation. Yet he acted where I hesitated. He chose principle over convenience, moral duty over financial interest. He did what I knew to be right and what I had counseled others to do, though I could not bring myself to do it.

But the emotion I feel is more complicated than simple shame, though shame is certainly present. There is something approaching wonder at the courage required for such a decision. I have spent my life in the world of politics, where one learns to calculate consequences, to weigh competing interests, to seek the possible rather than demand the perfect. Edward possessed what I perhaps lacked — the capacity for moral action that transcends calculation. He saw clearly what was required and he did it, without regard for the cost to himself or the inconvenience to his circumstances.

I am reminded of what I wrote to him when he first informed me of his purpose: "I have always found that the most difficult of all tasks is to do right in opposition to the current of opinion and interest." Edward accomplished that most difficult task. I did not. This is a judgment upon my character that I cannot escape and do not seek to evade.

Yet I confess there is also a measure of vicarious satisfaction in his achievement. If I could not myself perform the act of justice that my principles demanded, at least I had some small part in forming the mind that could perform it. The arguments I made to him about the evil of slavery, the principles I taught him about human equality and natural rights — these took root in soil more fertile than my own cautious nature provided. In Edward's courage, something of what I believed but could not enact found its expression in the world.

I think often of a letter he wrote to me from Illinois, describing the moment when he gathered his slaves and told them they were free. He wrote that their joy was beyond description, and that he felt for the first time in his life that he was truly living according to his conscience. I have never experienced such a moment. I have compromised with evil because I convinced myself that compromise was necessary, that gradual progress was preferable to immediate disruption, that my usefulness to the republic required the preservation of my estate and my standing in Virginia society.

These were not entirely false calculations. I did serve the cause of liberty in other ways — in the framing of the Constitution, in the defense of religious freedom, in the preservation of the Union. But in the matter that touched most closely upon the principles for which we fought the Revolution — the principle that all men are created equal — I failed where my young friend succeeded.

Edward Coles proved that it could be done. He proved that the obstacles I regarded as insurmountable were not insurmountable, merely difficult. He proved that a Virginia gentleman could free his slaves without destroying himself or abandoning his duty to society. In proving these things, he exposed the inadequacy of my own excuses.

I am proud of him as a father is proud of a son who surpasses him. I am grateful that principles I helped to plant bore fruit in a more courageous heart than my own. And I am humbled by the recognition that moral leadership sometimes passes from the teacher to the student, from the old to the young, from those who know what should be done to those who possess the will to do it.

This is perhaps the most painful aspect of my reflection upon Edward's example: the recognition that I knew better and did not do better. I cannot claim ignorance of the evil of slavery or uncertainty about the demands of justice. I understood both clearly. What I lacked was not knowledge but courage — the courage to act upon my convictions when action required sacrifice.

If there is consolation to be found in this comparison, it lies in the hope that others will follow Edward's example rather than mine. The principles of human equality and natural liberty are true whether I lived up to them or not. They found expression through Edward Coles, and they may find expression through others who possess what I did not — the moral courage to make their lives conform to their convictions, whatever the cost.
